headline: UK commits £65.5m development-fund subsidy to Viking CCS transport & storage cluster
summary: The UK Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) confirmed a £65.5 million, 2.5-year development-fund grant to Chrysaor Production UK Ltd (owned by Harbour Energy, and the Viking transport & storage developer), following Subsidy Advice Unit clearance, to de-risk and progress the Humber CCS cluster toward financial close. Viking is a BP/Harbour Track-2 project targeting CO2 storage in depleted southern North Sea gas fields.
co2crc_note: A government development-fund grant that de-risks a private T&S developer is a template for public co-funding of storage infrastructure economics relevant to Australian CCS hubs.
extractor_note: Grant pays on evidenced incurred costs over 2.5 years from April 2026; classed 'allocated' (awarded, not yet spent). Project storage-capacity figures in secondary coverage were inconsistent, so capacity_mtpa left null.

headline: PwC study for Gasunie finds CCS and low-carbon hydrogen the most cost-effective route to decarbonise Dutch industry
summary: A PwC study commissioned by Dutch gas-infrastructure operator Gasunie concludes that CCS, low-carbon (blue) hydrogen and hybrid electrification are the cheapest feasible routes to abate ~44 Mt CO2 from Dutch industry, with CCS alone covering ~21 Mtpa (about 18 Mtpa of it via blue hydrogen), conditional on timely infrastructure build-out. Government support was deliberately excluded to compare true technology cost.
co2crc_note: Independent economic evidence that CCS is the least-cost industrial-decarbonisation route reinforces the policy case CO2CRC makes for Australian storage.
extractor_note: Analysis/study item (no controlled instrument fits cleanly -> 'other'); Gasunie is a state-owned gas-transport infrastructure operator, org_type 'other'. The 21 Mtpa is a scenario figure, not a facility capacity, so capacity_mtpa left null.

headline: Hyundai and KNOC team up on cross-border CCS to store Korean CO2 in Southeast Asia
summary: Korea National Oil Corporation (KNOC) launched an international tender for a reservoir-development simulation study under an offshore CCS project run with an unnamed Southeast-Asian state oil company, to test permanent CO2 storage in depleted offshore fields; Hyundai E&C has been tapped to lead. A rare non-Western NOC, cross-border/transboundary catch surfaced by the IEAGHG weekly.
co2crc_note: A capture-in-one-country, store-in-another APAC arrangement is a direct analogue to Australia-Timor-Leste (Bayu-Undan) and a signpost for regional CO2-storage demand CO2CRC tracks.
extractor_note: Radar item (out of the 15-16 Jul window, published 14 Jul; <=7 days old). SE-Asian storage partner unnamed in source, so countries lists only South Korea. Hyundai E&C is the EPC contractor -> org_type 'developer'.
